policy_statement: Set standards for determining the GWP of eligible building materials in coordination with manufacturers, researchers, and other states/federal agencies.
mechanism_description: The interagency group works with manufacturers, researchers, and other governments to set standards for measuring building-material GWP, supporting lower-embodied-carbon material choices and reducing construction-related emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Building material specification/procurement standards → ↓ high-GWP material use + ↓ manufacturing process emissions → ↓ lifecycle GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: How building materials are quantified, selected, and potentially procured for construction (via standardized GWP measurement/verification), influencing designers, builders, and suppliers toward lower-embodied-carbon options.  
(2) Mediating process: Improved, comparable lifecycle assessment (LCA) accounting (e.g., EPD-based GWP) shifts market demand and can incentivize manufacturers to decarbonize production (fuel switching, efficiency, clinker substitution, recycled content, low-carbon steel routes).  
(3) Outcome: Lower embodied (Scope 3 and upstream) GHG emissions per unit of construction, reducing total construction-related emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced lifecycle GHG emissions from building materials (embodied carbon) due to measurement standards that drive adoption and production of lower-GWP materials.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism primarily reduces GHG emissions by lowering embodied carbon in construction materials through standardized measurement that enables/forces lower-GWP choices and industrial decarbonization.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: The principal effect is direct emissions reduction (CO₂e) rather than adaptation (risk reduction), resource efficiency as the main endpoint, or nature-based carbon sequestration. Resource efficiency can be a co-benefit (e.g., more recycled content), but the mechanism is explicitly keyed to GWP and emissions.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a “standard” were purely informational with no uptake, impacts could be limited; however, the dominant intended pathway across policies in this mechanism is still emissions mitigation via embodied-carbon reduction.  
4) Primary applies to ALL instances: Any policy centered on embodied-carbon/GWP standards for building materials has its main climate effect through reducing lifecycle GHG emissions associated with material production and use in construction.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether implementation is mainly via procurement mandates, disclosure-only requirements, or performance caps (and which materials: concrete/steel/asphalt/wood) is policy-specific and should be assigned at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some programs may function initially as measurement/disclosure infrastructure (enabling policy) with delayed emissions impacts; classification remains Mitigation because the mechanism’s climate-relevant objective and effect are embodied-emissions reductions rather than efficiency or adaptation.
key_indicators: "Develop standards"; "set standards for determining the GWP"; "eligible products"; "interagency group"; "work with manufacturers, trade associations, researchers"; "other states or federal agencies"
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as a planning/strategy or information-disclosure precursor, but the explicit action is standard-setting for GWP determination (a measurement/performance protocol) rather than disclosure requirements or procurement mandates.
